EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The coldest airmass since December of 1989 descended upon the Mid-South right before Christmas. Gusty north winds combined with very cold temperatures produced wind chills of 20 to 25 degrees below zero across West Tennessee from late Thursday, December 22nd into Friday, December 23rd. Temperatures dropped to between 0 and 5 degrees below zero during the morning of Friday, December 23rd. Numerous frozen pipes occurred in residential houses and businesses across the region. Wind chills remained below zero into the morning hours of December 24th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chill values fell to around 25 degrees below zero and low temperatures reached zero to 5 degrees below zero.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful arctic front swept through the Mid-South during the afternoon and evening on December 22nd. Temperatures dropped as much as 20 degrees in the first hour after the frontal passage. As temperatures plummeted rain changed to sleet and snow and quickly accumulated on area roads. A flash freeze occurred which resulted in very hazardous driving across West Tennesee with numerous accidents.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two to three inches of snow fell across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level ridge of high pressure parked over the Mid-South resulted in very hot and humid conditions during much of the month of June.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heat index values reached 105 degrees or greater.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Remnants of a mesoscale convective system moved across the northern portions of the Mid-South. This produced an outflow boundary that moved south into north Mississippi. It was the focus for severe thunderstorms that produced large hail and damaging wind.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Measured hail of 1 inch fell north of Pickwick Dam.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several rounds of  thunderstorms brought large hail and some damaging wind gusts to portions of northeast Tennessee, starting in the late evening hours of May 18 and continuing through the afternoon of May 19. Most of the reports were of large hail, with the largest being the size of a hen egg.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Surface low pressure moved from the Middle Mississippi Valley into the Great Lakes while a trailing cold front swept across the Mid-South on November 29, 2022. Clusters of storms developed during the late morning as a warm front lifted north ahead of the main system. Some of these storms were severe with large hail over northeast Mississippi. During the evening a second round of showers and thunderstorms developed and eventually formed a line that exited the region into Alabama by midnight.  This second round resulted in scattered severe storms with large hail and damaging winds across the Memphis metropolitan area and again across northeast Mississippi.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three inch tree limbs were broken and a few power poles were also broken.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system approaching middle Tennessee from the Great Plains along with moist southerly surface flow combined to produce scattered to numerous showers and thunderstorms over the area on October 12, 2022. Ahead of this approaching storm system, the atmosphere was supportive for strong to severe thunderstorms, especially across southern portions of middle Tennessee where modest instability was present along with strong wind shear values. This event produced widespread severe thunderstorm winds and wind damage with the most significant damage found in Manchester due to an intense microburst.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: About 15 reports of downed trees and power lines were received from Franklin to Brentwood. Damages are estimated.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm winds resulted in a 24-inch diameter maple tree downed onto a garage in Dunn, south of Lawrenceburg. Damages are estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system approaching middle Tennessee from the Great Plains along with moist southerly surface flow combined to produce scattered to numerous showers and thunderstorms over the area on October 12, 2022. Ahead of this approaching storm system, the atmosphere was supportive for strong to severe thunderstorms, especially across southern portions of middle Tennessee where modest instability was present along with strong wind shear values. This event produced widespread severe thunderstorm winds and wind damage with the most significant damage found in Manchester due to an intense microburst.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An intense microburst about one half mile long by 250 yards wide struck an area along Interstate Drive from around Monogard Drive southeastward to Highway 55. Sheet metal was blown off a building into a street sign on Monogard Drive. Large sections of the sheet metal roof were also blown off the Coffee Farmers Co-Op building on the west side of Interstate Drive, and a BP gas station canopy lost metal panels. On the east side of the roadway, one home lost a large section of its roof and a front porch column, and several trees were snapped and uprooted in the yard. Another gas station also suffered roof damage, and a nearby warehouse building lost part of its roof and had a cinder block wall collapse. Damage was blown from northwest to southeast, and winds were estimated up to 85 mph. Damages are a rough estimate.

Ahead of this approaching storm system, the atmosphere was supportive for strong to severe thunderstorms, especially across southern portions of middle Tennessee where modest instability was present along with strong wind shear values. This event produced widespread severe thunderstorm winds and wind damage with the most significant damage found in Manchester due to an intense microburst.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm winds resulted in several downed trees and power lines in the Henryville area. Damages are estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Due to an abnormally dry September and October, drought conditions returned in October 2022 to middle Tennessee. By the end of October, nearly 69 percent of middle Tennessee was in at least moderate drought with severe drought conditions stretching from Williamson County northeast into Sumner County. Severe drought conditions also developed in far northwest Stewart County. Precipitation totals across middle Tennessee generally ranged from 1 to 2.5 inches. The Nashville airport measured 1.38 inches of rain during October, well below the normal of 3.36 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe drought conditions developed across northern Williamson County in October 2022.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous thunderstorms affected much of Middle Tennessee during the early morning hours on January 1st, then again during the afternoon hours. These storms produced several reports of wind damage and one EF-1 tornado in Giles County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This tornado touched down in Limestone County, Alabama near the intersection of Persimmon Tree Road and Siniard Road, where a few trees were snapped or uprooted. It moved to the northeast, snapping and uprooting numerous trees as it crossed Tommy Hill Road and Lester Road. As the tornado crossed Holly Lane, it downed dozens of trees. It continued its northeastward progression, uprooting trees along Salem Minor Hill Road and Gardner Holly Road. It then continued into Giles County, Tennessee. Special thanks to the Limestone County EMA for their assistance with this survey. In Tennessee, the tornado continued northeast where NASA satellite imagery showed it blew down dozens of trees south of Hagan Road. On the eastern end of Hagan Road, two homes received minor damage and several outbuildings were destroyed. Another home had minor damage on Case Road at Bethel Road. The worst damage occurred on Alf Harris Road where several homes and outbuildings received significant damage. The tornado then turned more eastward and passed just north of the Prospect community, before more homes and outbuildings were damaged on Liberty Road, Kelley Road, and Prospect Elkton Road. The tornado lifted just west of Elkton, Tennessee. Total path length of this EF-1 tornado in Alabama and Tennessee was 16.9 miles. Special thanks to Giles County EMA for their help with this storm damage survey.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system tracked across West and Middle Tennessee into Kentucky and West Virginia through Sunday. Precipitation ahead of this system began as snow in the higher elevations of the Cumberland Plateau and mountains, while lower elevations began as rain. Behind the exiting system on Sunday night, a northwest flow developed and produced additional snow accumulations in the mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mt. Le Conte reported 14 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front pushed through the Mid-South early on January 2, 2022. Cold air filtered into the region throughout the day. A strong upper low moved into the area later in the day and interacted with a surface low forming along the Gulf coast. As a result, a band of snow developed across parts of West Tennessee and northeast Mississippi where one to three inches of snow fell causing some slippery roads and dangerous travel conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One to two inches of snow fell causing dangerous travel conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed along a warm front during the early morning hours of January 1, 2022. These storms developed across the northern sections of the Mid-South and a couple intensified over the Missouri Bootheel and northwest Tennessee causing large hail and damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree was knocked down on Cat Corner Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of mini supercells moved across the Memphis Metropolitan area during the early morning hours on New Year's Day. This convection was not tall enough to induce lightning but did produce a narrow swath of straight-line wind damage across East Memphis. Direct damage was primarily confined to trees although numerous homes sustained roof damage due to the falling trees. Wind damage was also reported in the Millington, Cordova, and Somerville areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The initial tree damage was noted just north of the intersection of North Rose Rd and Minden Rd. Damage was intermittent across the Hedge Hills neighborhood but became more widespread in the vicinity of Chickasaw Rd and Waring Rd. Large trees were uprooted and fell on several homes in the area, resulting in significant roof damage. This pattern of damage continued east along Chickasaw Rd across Perkins Rd with several more homes suffering significant roof damage from falling trees along Erwin Dr and Vescovo Dr just north of Chickasaw Rd. The damage became much more intermittent as the storm moved past White Station Elementary School with only minor tree damage noted between Avon Road and Interstate 240. The last known damage was found along the Shelby Farms Greenline just east of the Wolf River.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moving up the spine of the Appalachians brought a period of heavy snowfall to the northern Cumberland Plateau area during the day.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system tracked across West and Middle Tennessee into Kentucky and West Virginia through Sunday. Precipitation ahead of this system began as snow in the higher elevations of the Cumberland Plateau and mountains, while lower elevations began as rain. Behind the exiting system on Sunday night, a northwest flow developed and produced additional snow accumulations in the mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Near Elgin, 8.5 inches of snow was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of strong to marginally severe thunderstorms moved across Middle Tennessee during the afternoon and evening hours on February 17. A few reports of wind damage and flooding were received. Ahead of the line of storms, strong south winds occurred across Middle Tennessee throughout the day, with peak wind gusts measured over 50 mph in some locations. Some of the highest measured wind gusts included 54 mph at a home weather station in Crossville at 1145 AM CDT; 52 mph at a CWOP station on Short Mountain at 949 AM CDT; 52 mph at the ASOS at Nashville International Airport at 400 PM CDT; and 51 mph at the ASOS at Clarksville Outlaw Field at 222 AM CDT. Despite these strong south winds, no notable wind damage reports were received.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: AWOS station KSYI at Shelbyville Bomar Field measured a peak wind gust of 58 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Light freezing rain affected the far northwest portion of Middle Tennessee during the morning hours on February 24. Up to one tenth of an inch of ice accumulated on elevated surfaces across Stewart County, such as trees and power lines, which resulted in some scattered power outages but no travel impacts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to one tenth of an inch of ice accumulated on elevated surfaces across Stewart County, such as trees and power lines, which resulted in some scattered power outages. However, there was no accumulation on roads and no travel impacts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Light snow fell across parts of eastern Middle Tennessee during the late evening hours on February 17 into the early morning hours of February 18. Up to an inch of snow was reported on the Cumberland Plateau, but most areas only received a trace of accumulation, if any.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of strong to marginally severe thunderstorms moved across Middle Tennessee during the afternoon and evening hours on February 17. A few reports of wind damage and flooding were received. Ahead of the line of storms, strong south winds occurred across Middle Tennessee throughout the day, with peak wind gusts measured over 50 mph in some locations. Some of the highest measured wind gusts included 54 mph at a home weather station in Crossville at 1145 AM CDT; 52 mph at a CWOP station on Short Mountain at 949 AM CDT; 52 mph at the ASOS at Nashville International Airport at 400 PM CDT; and 51 mph at the ASOS at Clarksville Outlaw Field at 222 AM CDT. Despite these strong south winds, no notable wind damage reports were received.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Empson Drive in Greenbrier was closed due to flooding. Other roads were flooded and closed in White House and Springfield.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic front slowly pushed across the Mid-South on Wednesday, February 2, 2022. This front edged south into northeast Mississippi by 12 am Thursday, February 3, 2022, and then stalled. Arctic air poured into the Mid-South with patchy light freezing rain across northeast Arkansas and the Missouri Bootheel overnight. A potent upper-level disturbance moved into the region and interacted with the stalled front starting early Thursday morning. A surface low developed and moved east-northeast along the front along with a shield of heavy wintry precipitation. The cold air deepened quickly across the Delta region of the Missouri Bootheel, eastern Arkansas, and extreme northwest Tennessee. The region experienced mostly sleet from this event with a widespread amount of one to two inches and isolated three-inch amounts. Transportation impacts were extensive. A corridor of heavy freezing rain was located from Phillips County, AR, through extreme northwest Mississippi, and across much of West Tennessee from Memphis to Brownsville to Paris. These areas received from one-quarter inch to three-quarters inch of ice. Significant damage occurred to the power supply infrastructure throughout this region due to widespread trees and power lines being knocked down. Light icing occurred across other parts of northwest Mississippi such as Clarksdale and along I-40 in West Tennessee to the east of Jackson, Tennessee.  Heavy rain fell along and south of the stalled arctic front across much of north Mississippi. Precipitation tapered off from west to east during the evening hours of Thursday, February 3, 2022.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Around one-third inch of ice led to widespread tree and powerline damage along with power outages.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front sagged southward through the Mid-South on Tuesday, February 22, 2022, eventually stalling across central Mississippi by Wednesday, February 23, 2022. Cold air gradually deepened across northern sections of the Mid-South. A potent upper-level trough approached the region Wednesday afternoon resulting in a burst of sleet across northeast Arkansas and the Missouri Bootheel which quickly impacted travel.  Moisture continued to stream across the region Wednesday night into Thursday morning as cold air seeped south toward the I-40 corridor in Arkansas and across northwest Tennessee. This resulted in a corridor of heavy icing across much of east Arkansas and northwest Tennessee. One-quarter to one-half inch of ice resulted in widespread power outages caused by tree and powerline damage. Lighter amounts of icing occurred across other parts of West Tennessee including Memphis, Paris, and Lexington. Precipitation tapered off during the afternoon hours on Thursday, February 24, 2022.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Around one-eighth of an inch of ice led to some tree limbs down.

Lighter amounts of icing occurred across other parts of West Tennessee including Memphis, Paris, and Lexington. Precipitation tapered off during the afternoon hours on Thursday, February 24, 2022.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to two-tenths of an inch of ice led to some tree limbs down and isolated power outages.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After severe storms affected Middle Tennessee on January 1, light rain moved back across Middle Tennessee during the late morning hours on January 2. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another widespread snow event affected Middle Tennessee during the day on Thursday, January 6, 2022, just 4 days after a previous significant snowfall. Many locations received several inches of snowfall, especially areas along and north of I-40. South of I-40, particularly near the Alabama border, warmer air aloft kept precipitation as a wintry mix of rain, freezing rain, sleet, and snow, with much lower accumulations of snow along with a light glaze of ice. With temperatures well below freezing throughout Middle Tennessee, hazardous travel conditions developed that continued into the following Saturday, resulting in the temporary closure of nearly all interstates, as well as nearly all school districts shutting down until the following week.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to 5 inches of snow fell across the county. CoCoRaHS station Dover 7.8 NNE measured 4.2 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic front slowly pushed across the Mid-South on Wednesday, February 2, 2022. This front edged south into northeast Mississippi by 12 am Thursday, February 3, 2022, and then stalled. Arctic air poured into the Mid-South with patchy light freezing rain across northeast Arkansas and the Missouri Bootheel overnight. A potent upper-level disturbance moved into the region and interacted with the stalled front starting early Thursday morning. A surface low developed and moved east-northeast along the front along with a shield of heavy wintry precipitation. The cold air deepened quickly across the Delta region of the Missouri Bootheel, eastern Arkansas, and extreme northwest Tennessee. The region experienced mostly sleet from this event with a widespread amount of one to two inches and isolated three-inch amounts. Transportation impacts were extensive. A corridor of heavy freezing rain was located from Phillips County, AR, through extreme northwest Mississippi, and across much of West Tennessee from Memphis to Brownsville to Paris. These areas received from one-quarter inch to three-quarters inch of ice. Significant damage occurred to the power supply infrastructure throughout this region due to widespread trees and power lines being knocked down. Light icing occurred across other parts of northwest Mississippi such as Clarksdale and along I-40 in West Tennessee to the east of Jackson, Tennessee.  Heavy rain fell along and south of the stalled arctic front across much of north Mississippi. Precipitation tapered off from west to east during the evening hours of Thursday, February 3, 2022.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One-quarter inch of ice led to widespread tree and powerline damage and power outages.

South of I-40, particularly near the Alabama border, warmer air aloft kept precipitation as a wintry mix of rain, freezing rain, sleet, and snow, with much lower accumulations of snow along with a light glaze of ice. With temperatures well below freezing throughout Middle Tennessee, hazardous travel conditions developed that continued into the following Saturday, resulting in the temporary closure of nearly all interstates, as well as nearly all school districts shutting down until the following week.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to 7 inches of snow fell across the county. CoCoRaHS station Lebanon 10.5 ENE measured 7.0 inches of snow, CoCoRaHS station Green Hill 0.3 N measured 6.5 inches of snow, and CoCoRaHS station Mount Juliet 2.9 WSW measured 6.0 inches of snow. NWS Nashville measured 6.5 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front sagged southward through the Mid-South on Tuesday, February 22, 2022, eventually stalling across central Mississippi by Wednesday, February 23, 2022. Cold air gradually deepened across northern sections of the Mid-South. A potent upper-level trough approached the region Wednesday afternoon resulting in a burst of sleet across northeast Arkansas and the Missouri Bootheel which quickly impacted travel.  Moisture continued to stream across the region Wednesday night into Thursday morning as cold air seeped south toward the I-40 corridor in Arkansas and across northwest Tennessee. This resulted in a corridor of heavy icing across much of east Arkansas and northwest Tennessee. One-quarter to one-half inch of ice resulted in widespread power outages caused by tree and powerline damage. Lighter amounts of icing occurred across other parts of West Tennessee including Memphis, Paris, and Lexington. Precipitation tapered off during the afternoon hours on Thursday, February 24, 2022.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to one-third of an inch of ice led to widespread tree and power line damage along with power outages. Over 100 trees had fallen due to ice with nearly 40 downed power lines. Three vehicle crashes occurred. One crash involved a fatality when a falling limb hit a vehicle.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic front slowly pushed across the Mid-South on Wednesday, February 2, 2022. This front edged south into northeast Mississippi by 12 am Thursday, February 3, 2022, and then stalled. Arctic air poured into the Mid-South with patchy light freezing rain across northeast Arkansas and the Missouri Bootheel overnight. A potent upper-level disturbance moved into the region and interacted with the stalled front starting early Thursday morning. A surface low developed and moved east-northeast along the front along with a shield of heavy wintry precipitation. The cold air deepened quickly across the Delta region of the Missouri Bootheel, eastern Arkansas, and extreme northwest Tennessee. The region experienced mostly sleet from this event with a widespread amount of one to two inches and isolated three-inch amounts. Transportation impacts were extensive. A corridor of heavy freezing rain was located from Phillips County, AR, through extreme northwest Mississippi, and across much of West Tennessee from Memphis to Brownsville to Paris. These areas received from one-quarter inch to three-quarters inch of ice. Significant damage occurred to the power supply infrastructure throughout this region due to widespread trees and power lines being knocked down. Light icing occurred across other parts of northwest Mississippi such as Clarksdale and along I-40 in West Tennessee to the east of Jackson, Tennessee.  Heavy rain fell along and south of the stalled arctic front across much of north Mississippi. Precipitation tapered off from west to east during the evening hours of Thursday, February 3, 2022.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to three inches of sleet resulted in widespread travel disruption. Around one-tenth inch of ice led to some tree limbs down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level trough developed over the Desert Southwest. A surface low over the Texas/ Oklahoma region moved northeastward in conjunction with the trough. Ahead of this system, strong southerly winds had developed due to a tight pressure gradient. A cold front then moved across the Mid-South with gusty wind and severe thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A couple of trees were knocked down on Highway 54 North near Brownsville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous showers and thunderstorms affected Middle Tennessee on February 22. Some of the storms became severe with a few reports of large hail and wind damage. In addition, heavy rainfall resulted in flooding across several areas of Middle Tennessee. Rainfall totals from February 21 to February 25 ranged from 3 to over 7 inches fell across the area, which led to widespread river flooding on most rivers and streams across the region through the rest of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down on Batson Road on the Louise Creek bridge near Highway 48.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front pushed through the Mid-South early on January 2, 2022. Cold air filtered into the region throughout the day. A strong upper low moved into the area later in the day and interacted with a surface low forming along the Gulf coast. As a result, a band of snow developed across parts of West Tennessee and northeast Mississippi where one to three inches of snow fell causing some slippery roads and dangerous travel conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two to three inches of snow fell causing dangerous travel conditions.

As a result, a band of snow developed across parts of West Tennessee and northeast Mississippi where one to three inches of snow fell causing some slippery roads and dangerous travel conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two to three inches of snow fell causing dangerous travel conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another widespread snow event affected Middle Tennessee during the day on Thursday, January 6, 2022, just 4 days after a previous significant snowfall. Many locations received several inches of snowfall, especially areas along and north of I-40. South of I-40, particularly near the Alabama border, warmer air aloft kept precipitation as a wintry mix of rain, freezing rain, sleet, and snow, with much lower accumulations of snow along with a light glaze of ice. With temperatures well below freezing throughout Middle Tennessee, hazardous travel conditions developed that continued into the following Saturday, resulting in the temporary closure of nearly all interstates, as well as nearly all school districts shutting down until the following week.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to 5 inches of snow fell across the county. CoCoRaHS station Crossville 4.6 NNW measured 4.1 inches of snow, CoCoRaHS station Crossville 0.9 S measured 2.0 inches of snow, and CoCoRaHS station Crossville 6.9 S measured 1.0 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic front slowly pushed across the Mid-South on Wednesday, February 2, 2022. This front edged south into northeast Mississippi by 12 am Thursday, February 3, 2022, and then stalled. Arctic air poured into the Mid-South with patchy light freezing rain across northeast Arkansas and the Missouri Bootheel overnight. A potent upper-level disturbance moved into the region and interacted with the stalled front starting early Thursday morning. A surface low developed and moved east-northeast along the front along with a shield of heavy wintry precipitation. The cold air deepened quickly across the Delta region of the Missouri Bootheel, eastern Arkansas, and extreme northwest Tennessee. The region experienced mostly sleet from this event with a widespread amount of one to two inches and isolated three-inch amounts. Transportation impacts were extensive. A corridor of heavy freezing rain was located from Phillips County, AR, through extreme northwest Mississippi, and across much of West Tennessee from Memphis to Brownsville to Paris. These areas received from one-quarter inch to three-quarters inch of ice. Significant damage occurred to the power supply infrastructure throughout this region due to widespread trees and power lines being knocked down. Light icing occurred across other parts of northwest Mississippi such as Clarksdale and along I-40 in West Tennessee to the east of Jackson, Tennessee.  Heavy rain fell along and south of the stalled arctic front across much of north Mississippi. Precipitation tapered off from west to east during the evening hours of Thursday, February 3, 2022.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to three inches of sleet resulted in widespread travel disruption. One-quarter inch of ice led to widespread tree and powerline damage and power outages.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous showers and thunderstorms affected Middle Tennessee on February 22. Some of the storms became severe with a few reports of large hail and wind damage. In addition, heavy rainfall resulted in flooding across several areas of Middle Tennessee. Rainfall totals from February 21 to February 25 ranged from 3 to over 7 inches fell across the area, which led to widespread river flooding on most rivers and streams across the region through the rest of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water covered Highway 49 just south of Dover in at least two locations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front sagged southward through the Mid-South on Tuesday, February 22, 2022, eventually stalling across central Mississippi by Wednesday, February 23, 2022. Cold air gradually deepened across northern sections of the Mid-South. A potent upper-level trough approached the region Wednesday afternoon resulting in a burst of sleet across northeast Arkansas and the Missouri Bootheel which quickly impacted travel.  Moisture continued to stream across the region Wednesday night into Thursday morning as cold air seeped south toward the I-40 corridor in Arkansas and across northwest Tennessee. This resulted in a corridor of heavy icing across much of east Arkansas and northwest Tennessee. One-quarter to one-half inch of ice resulted in widespread power outages caused by tree and powerline damage. Lighter amounts of icing occurred across other parts of West Tennessee including Memphis, Paris, and Lexington. Precipitation tapered off during the afternoon hours on Thursday, February 24, 2022.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to one-half inch of sleet resulted in widespread travel disruption. Around two-tenth of an inch of ice resulted in some tree limbs down and isolated power outages.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful arctic front swept through the Mid-South during the afternoon and evening on December 22nd. Temperatures dropped as much as 20 degrees in the first hour after the frontal passage. As temperatures plummeted rain changed to sleet and snow and quickly accumulated on area roads. A flash freeze occurred which resulted in very hazardous driving across West Tennesee with numerous accidents.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Around one inch of snow fell across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another widespread snow event affected Middle Tennessee during the day on Thursday, January 6, 2022, just 4 days after a previous significant snowfall. Many locations received several inches of snowfall, especially areas along and north of I-40. South of I-40, particularly near the Alabama border, warmer air aloft kept precipitation as a wintry mix of rain, freezing rain, sleet, and snow, with much lower accumulations of snow along with a light glaze of ice. With temperatures well below freezing throughout Middle Tennessee, hazardous travel conditions developed that continued into the following Saturday, resulting in the temporary closure of nearly all interstates, as well as nearly all school districts shutting down until the following week.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to 5 inches of snow fell across the county. Social media reports indicated 5 inches of snow fell in Readyville, and 3 inches of snow fell in Woodbury..

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic front slowly pushed across the Mid-South on Wednesday, February 2, 2022. This front edged south into northeast Mississippi by 12 am Thursday, February 3, 2022, and then stalled. Arctic air poured into the Mid-South with patchy light freezing rain across northeast Arkansas and the Missouri Bootheel overnight. A potent upper-level disturbance moved into the region and interacted with the stalled front starting early Thursday morning. A surface low developed and moved east-northeast along the front along with a shield of heavy wintry precipitation. The cold air deepened quickly across the Delta region of the Missouri Bootheel, eastern Arkansas, and extreme northwest Tennessee. The region experienced mostly sleet from this event with a widespread amount of one to two inches and isolated three-inch amounts. Transportation impacts were extensive. A corridor of heavy freezing rain was located from Phillips County, AR, through extreme northwest Mississippi, and across much of West Tennessee from Memphis to Brownsville to Paris. These areas received from one-quarter inch to three-quarters inch of ice. Significant damage occurred to the power supply infrastructure throughout this region due to widespread trees and power lines being knocked down. Light icing occurred across other parts of northwest Mississippi such as Clarksdale and along I-40 in West Tennessee to the east of Jackson, Tennessee.  Heavy rain fell along and south of the stalled arctic front across much of north Mississippi. Precipitation tapered off from west to east during the evening hours of Thursday, February 3, 2022.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to one-half inch of ice resulted in widespread tree and powerline damage and power outages.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front sagged southward through the Mid-South on Tuesday, February 22, 2022, eventually stalling across central Mississippi by Wednesday, February 23, 2022. Cold air gradually deepened across northern sections of the Mid-South. A potent upper-level trough approached the region Wednesday afternoon resulting in a burst of sleet across northeast Arkansas and the Missouri Bootheel which quickly impacted travel.  Moisture continued to stream across the region Wednesday night into Thursday morning as cold air seeped south toward the I-40 corridor in Arkansas and across northwest Tennessee. This resulted in a corridor of heavy icing across much of east Arkansas and northwest Tennessee. One-quarter to one-half inch of ice resulted in widespread power outages caused by tree and powerline damage. Lighter amounts of icing occurred across other parts of West Tennessee including Memphis, Paris, and Lexington. Precipitation tapered off during the afternoon hours on Thursday, February 24, 2022.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Around one-quarter inch of ice led to widespread tree and power line damage along with power outages.

A flash freeze occurred which resulted in very hazardous driving across West Tennesee with numerous accidents.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous supercell thunderstorms tracked northeast across East Tennessee, starting in the late morning hours and continuing into the evening. Many of the storms produced damaging wind gusts and large hail, mainly in central and northern portions of the Tennessee Valley. Although several storms exhibited rotation signatures on radar, no tornadoes were confirmed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The coldest airmass since December of 1989 descended upon the Mid-South right before Christmas. Gusty north winds combined with very cold temperatures produced wind chills of 20 to 25 degrees below zero across West Tennessee from late Thursday, December 22nd into Friday, December 23rd. Temperatures dropped to between 0 and 5 degrees below zero during the morning of Friday, December 23rd. Numerous frozen pipes occurred in residential houses and businesses across the region. Wind chills remained below zero into the morning hours of December 24th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chill values remained below zero.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic front moved through the Mid-South during the day on March 11, 2022. Cold air filtered into the region. Meanwhile, a potent upper-level disturbance tracked from the southwest US into the Mid-South. Precipitation spread into the region during the late afternoon and early evening hours and quickly changed over to snow and became heavy at times over much of the Mid-South. Up to 7 inches of snow fell in some locations resulting in difficult travel conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to 4 inches of snow fell.

An arctic front moved through the Mid-South during the day on March 11, 2022. Cold air filtered into the region. Meanwhile, a potent upper-level disturbance tracked from the southwest US into the Mid-South. Precipitation spread into the region during the late afternoon and early evening hours and quickly changed over to snow and became heavy at times over much of the Mid-South. Up to 7 inches of snow fell in some locations resulting in difficult travel conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Around 2 inches of snow fell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The coldest airmass since December of 1989 descended upon the Mid-South right before Christmas. Gusty north winds combined with very cold temperatures produced wind chills of 20 to 25 degrees below zero across West Tennessee from late Thursday, December 22nd into Friday, December 23rd. Temperatures dropped to between 0 and 5 degrees below zero during the morning of Friday, December 23rd. Numerous frozen pipes occurred in residential houses and businesses across the region. Wind chills remained below zero into the morning hours of December 24th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chill values remained below zero.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful arctic front swept through the Mid-South during the afternoon and evening on December 22nd. Temperatures dropped as much as 20 degrees in the first hour after the frontal passage. As temperatures plummeted rain changed to sleet and snow and quickly accumulated on area roads. A flash freeze occurred which resulted in very hazardous driving across West Tennesee with numerous accidents.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Around one inch of snow fell across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of severe thunderstorms moved across Middle Tennessee during the late afternoon and evening hours on April 13, 2022. Widespread damaging winds affected most counties of Middle Tennessee, with numerous trees and power lines blown down along with widely scattered structural damage. Winds were measured up to nearly 70 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and power lines were blown down in the St. Bethlehem area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Surface low pressure moved from the Middle Mississippi Valley into the Great Lakes while a trailing cold front swept across the Mid-South on November 29, 2022. Clusters of storms developed during the late morning as a warm front lifted north ahead of the main system. Some of these storms were severe with large hail over northeast Mississippi. During the evening a second round of showers and thunderstorms developed and eventually formed a line that exited the region into Alabama by midnight.  This second round resulted in scattered severe storms with large hail and damaging winds across the Memphis metropolitan area and again across northeast Mississippi.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter-sized hail fell near Shelby Drive and Lamar Avenue.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Surface low pressure moved from the Middle Mississippi Valley into the Great Lakes while a trailing cold front swept across the Mid-South on November 29, 2022. Clusters of storms developed during the late morning as a warm front lifted north ahead of the main system. Some of these storms were severe with large hail over northeast Mississippi. During the evening a second round of showers and thunderstorms developed and eventually formed a line that exited the region into Alabama by midnight.  This second round resulted in scattered severe storms with large hail and damaging winds across the Memphis metropolitan area and again across northeast Mississippi.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Estimated hail of 0.75 inches fell two miles southeast of Memphis.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave moved into the Lower Mississippi Valley triggering numerous showers and thunderstorms across Arkansas during the morning hours of April 13, 2022. Storms eventually organized into a line that swept across the Mid-South. A strong low-level jet, large amounts of wind shear, and moderate amounts of instability supported severe thunderstorms across the Mid-South. Large hail, damaging wind and tornadoes were the result. Locally heavy rain also caused flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees damaged along Milltown Road.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Drone video footage, social media reports and photos indicate a 1/2 mile long by 1/2 mile wide microburst struck the area around Highway 70 at Hollis Creek Road. Beans Diesel Performance Shop had part of the roof blown off, and a storage barn was completely destroyed at a lumber yard across the highway. Debris from the storage barn was blown up to 300 yards away. Two outbuildings were also destroyed on Haley Lane and Hollis Creek Road, and around two dozen trees were snapped or uprooted in the area. Winds were estimated up to 80 mph. Special thanks to @cannonseverewx for assistance with this storm survey.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved through the Mid-South on May 1, 2022, and stalled over central Mississippi. It then moved back north, into the Mid-South, as a warm front on May 2, 2022. Southerly flow, a warm and moist air mass, coupled with an upper-level disturbance moving over the region produced showers and thunderstorms. Damaging wind and large hail were the main hazards.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter-sized hail fell two miles northwest of Mifflin.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A QLCS moved across Middle Tennessee during the afternoon and evening hours on March 22, producing a few reports of wind damage. Ahead of the QLCS, strong south winds occurred across the region with wind gusts near and over 50 mph at times, although no damage was reported. A wind gust of 53 mph was measured at the Clarksville Outlaw Field ASOS at 853pm CDT, and a wind gust of 47 mph was measured at the Nashville International Airport ASOS at 1148pm CDT.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tennessee Valley Weather and Facebook reports, along with radar and satellite data, indicate a swath of wind damage occurred between Collinwood and Waynesboro. Numerous trees and power lines were blown down with power outages along Waterfall Creek Road, Chalk Creek Road, Sunny Acres Drive, and Johnson Hollow Road. A lumber yard on Chalk Creek Road also suffered minor damage. In addition, NASA satellite imagery indicates a 200 yard wide swath of trees was blown down in rural forests from Waterfall Creek Road to Chalk Creek Road, suggesting this may have been a weak QLCS tornado. However, the damage along with satellite and radar data are too inconclusive at this time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A QLCS moved across all of Middle Tennessee during the afternoon and evening hours on March 30, producing several reports of wind damage. Measured peak wind gusts included 56 mph at the Clarksville Outlaw Field ASOS at 536pm CDT, 55 mph at the Nashville International Airport ASOS at 758pm CDT, and 55 mph at the Shelbyville Bomar Field AWOS at 815pm CDT.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were blown down across western parts of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system tracking across the southeastern states brought an anomalously late season heavy snowfall event to the Tennessee Valley and southern Appalachian region. This was the most significant widespread March winter event in the region since the '93 Storm of the Century. This event also produced the most single-day snowfall at the Knoxville climate site (6.5) since the 1993 storm. The combination of high snowfall rates and strong winds led to scattered power outages, in addition to difficult travel.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow amounts between 4 and 7 inches were reported across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful low pressure system moved northeast from the central Plains into the Great Lakes, bringing a strong cold front through the Mississippi and Tennessee Valleys. Very windy conditions developed during the afternoon hours of the 30th, producing sustained speeds of 25-35 mph and gusts of 45 to 50 mph. These winds knocked down numerous tree limbs and a few trees, resulting in a few power lines being compromised and power outages in portions of southern middle Tennessee. Isolated roof damage also occurred.||A squall line which originated during the early morning of the 30th over Arkansas reached northwest Alabama around 5-7 PM, and tracked east across southern middle Tennessee during the evening hours. The line of thunderstorms also produced sporadic wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds of 25 to 35 mph with gusts up to 50 mph affected Franklin county, TN. Some trees were blown down and roof damage occurred at the Franklin county EMA office.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After temperatures reached the middle to upper 60s, a strong cold front arrived during the evening hours of the 11th dropping temperatures sharply by 20 to 30 degrees and into the 20s in just a few hours. This was followed by a band of rain that quickly changed to snow which accumulated to 2 to 4 inches at many locations in southern middle Tennessee in just a few hours during the late evening of the 11th into the early morning of the 12th. Gusty northwest winds of 15 to 25 mph gusting to around 30 mph persisted into the morning hours with a few flurries and light snow showers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulations of 2.2 to 3.0 inches were reported in the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system tracking across the southeastern states brought an anomalously late season heavy snowfall event to the Tennessee Valley and southern Appalachian region. This was the most significant widespread March winter event in the region since the '93 Storm of the Century. This event also produced the most single-day snowfall at the Knoxville climate site (6.5) since the 1993 storm. The combination of high snowfall rates and strong winds led to scattered power outages, in addition to difficult travel.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow amounts between 3 and 6 inches were reported across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After some overnight thunderstorms caused wind damage in Robertson County, additional scattered showers and storms developed across Middle Tennessee during the afternoon and evening hours on June 7. Several reports of large hail and flash flooding were received.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck and damaged a home at 405 Osborne Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe MCS moved southeastward across Middle Tennessee during the morning and early afternoon hours on June 17, producing widespread damaging winds and several reports of large hail. This was the second largest severe weather event of the 2022 spring season. Some measured wind gusts included 63 mph at Clarksville Outlaw Field, 53 mph at Springfield Airport, 48 mph at Nashville Int'l Airport, 52 mph at Smyrna Airport, and 61 mph at Murfreesboro Airport.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree fell onto the porch of a house in the 600 block of Lucas Road, pinning a women underneath and causing her serious injuries. She was hospitalized for several days.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mesoscale convective system (MCS) moved through the Ozarks and into the Mid-South on the morning of June 6th. Numerous showers and thunderstorms moved across the Mid-South during the morning hours. This activity weakened and produced outflow boundaries. These boundaries interacted with an increasingly moist and unstable atmosphere and by early evening convection had redeveloped. This resulted in numerous showers and thunderstorms with heavy rainfall, damaging wind, and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large tree limbs snapped, large trees uprooted. Carport destroyed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mesoscale convective system (MCS) moved through the Ozarks and into the Mid-South on the morning of June 6th. Numerous showers and thunderstorms moved across the Mid-South during the morning hours. This activity weakened and produced outflow boundaries. These boundaries interacted with an increasingly moist and unstable atmosphere and by early evening convection had redeveloped. This resulted in numerous showers and thunderstorms with heavy rainfall, damaging wind, and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado briefly touched down in the Christmasville community in Haywood County. The tornado caused roof damage to the Hickory Grove Missionary Baptist Church. A cemetery next to the church had several small headstones knocked down. One large tree along with several large tree limbs was blown down.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A NWS storm survey determined a west-southwestward moving severe thunderstorm produced a significant 1.5 mile long by 0.75 mile wide microburst across northern Livingston. Damage began on the northeast side of Livingston near School Mountain Road at Highway 111 then continued west-southwestward through the northern side of the city, where multiple trees were uprooted, tin was peeled off the metal roof of a house, and a few trees fell onto vehicles on Kennedy Street and Buena Vista Road. Multiple trees were also uprooted and a couple of trees were snapped along Greenfield Drive and Hillcrest Drive, with one tree falling through a house on Greenfield Drive. Damage totals reached 1.6 million dollars per the Livingston mayor. Winds were estimated up to 70 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful arctic front swept through the Mid-South during the afternoon and evening on December 22nd. Temperatures dropped as much as 20 degrees in the first hour after the frontal passage. As temperatures plummeted rain changed to sleet and snow and quickly accumulated on area roads. A flash freeze occurred which resulted in very hazardous driving across West Tennesee with numerous accidents.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two to three inches of snow fell across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On Wednesday 03/30/2022 a strong shortwave trough and cold front moved across the region. Strong gradient winds occurred before the cold front moved across the area. Severe thunderstorms, flooding and tornadoes occurred. Damage was reported across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado caused intermittent damage along a seven mile path that continued into southeast Gibson County. The tornado formed just west of the intersection of Barnes Road and Old Medina Road near the railroad tracks. The most significant damage occurred in this area where two homes sustained considerable damage. One home lost most of its roof and the east-facing garage door was blown in. A nearby metal building suffered significant damage. The second home sustained roof damage and many windows were blown out on the north- and south-facing walls. Debris was lofted and deposited in the forest downstream. A couple of barns were damaged farther east-northeast along Barnes Road with intermittent tree damage as the tornado traveled northeast. The next area of damage was at the intersection of Friendship Road and Barnes Road. A church and nearby home suffered minor roof damage and numerous trees were downed. Additional damage was noted on Christmasville Road in Madison County and then the tornado moved into Gibson County. Peak winds were estimated at 110 mph in Madison County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On Wednesday 03/30/2022 a strong shortwave trough and cold front moved across the region. Strong gradient winds occurred before the cold front moved across the area. Severe thunderstorms, flooding and tornadoes occurred. Damage was reported across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado developed just south of Womack Lane to the west of  Denmark-Jackson Road. Damage was observed to two homes on Womack  Lane, one with significant roof damage. Numerous trees were uprooted or snapped as this tornado moved northeast across rural, wooded land. Additional damage was observed at the Pacific  Manufacturing facility on Smith Lane where the east-facing wall of a large metal building was peeled off of the frame. Several large rolling doors were blown in at this facility. There were also several metal power poles that were severely bent along Smith Lane. The tornado continued northeast to the south of the McKellar-Sipes Airport.   Trees were uprooted along Old Denmark Road and a house suffered roof damage.  Several businesses were damaged along Airways Boulevard just to the west of the South Fork of the Forked Deer River.  The tornado moved into the southwest portion of Jackson uprooting trees, knocking down powerlines, and damaging the Jackson-Madison County General Hospital.  A light pole was damaged at a baseball field. The roof came off at the Forrest Cove Nursing Home near the hospital before the tornado lifted. Peak winds were estimated at 100 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong system and associated cold front over the Great Plains weakened considerably as it approached middle Tennessee on November 5, 2022, due to a general lack of instability across the area. Still, a strengthening surface pressure gradient ahead of this storm system allowed for winds to increase throughout much of the day ahead of the line of rain showers. Wind gusts between 35 to 50 mph were common, and this resulted in a few reports of downed trees and tree limbs as well as power outages, particularly across the Nashville metro area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Twitter tSpotter report indicated a carport collapsed on Hoggett Ford Road in southern Hermitage. Damages are estimated for the carport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Abnormally dry conditions during the middle part of the summer led to severe (D2) drought conditions over portions of Tennesee during the month of July. A semi-permanent upper ridge of high pressure resulted in little rainfall. The drought created stress on crops|including corn and soybeans which led to lower yields. In addition, the drought caused river and lake levels to be at low levels.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe (D2) drought conditions developed during the month of July.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lack of rainfall across the region during June 2022 led to moderate to severe drought conditions developing across western and southern Middle Tennessee throughout the month of July 2022. Drought conditions were worst in the counties along the Tennessee River. However, drought improved in some areas due to plentiful rainfall in mid and late July 2022, especially around the Nashville metro. As of September 2022, no damage figures are available due to the drought conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With much of the month of November 2022 remaining abnormally dry, drought conditions across Middle Tennessee worsened. By the end of November, 100 percent of Middle Tennessee was in at least moderate drought with about 34 percent in severe drought. Drought conditions worsened the most for counties near the Tennessee River and those along and near the Cumberland Plateau. For much of the month, precipitation totals were well below normal, generally ranging from 1 to 3 inches. A beneficial storm system brought widespread rainfall during the last couple days of November that should help improve drought conditions in the next updates in December.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe drought conditions developed across Grundy County in November 2022.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large storm system stretching from the mid-Mississippi Valley northward into the Great Lakes region on September 2nd through September 4, 2022, along with an abundant amount of moisture resulted in scattered to numerous showers and thunderstorms across middle Tennessee. Due to slow storm motions and training of thunderstorms, heavy rain and areas of flash flooding were the primary threat during this event with impacts mainly felt near and south of Interstate 40. Several reports of flash flooding were received along with heavy rainfall totals of 3 to 6 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reports and photos shared on Facebook and Twitter showed street flooding along Stewart Creek Road near Knob Road in Nolensville with flood waters up to some homes along the roadway.

Several reports of flash flooding were received along with heavy rainfall totals of 3 to 6 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Facebook report showed flooding at the Brookwood Apartments in Columbia on West 7th Street and Academy Lane with several cars flooded. Damages are estimated for the flooded cars.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a cold front tracked eastward across the Great Plains and moist, southerly flow set up over the southeastern United States, a severe weather event set up over this area, including portions of Middle Tennessee, on November 29, 2022. Despite an abundant amount of wind shear and modest mid-level lapse rates, the greatest amount of instability remained to the south of Middle Tennessee. Thus, thunderstorms across the area generally remained sub-severe with just beneficial rainfall observed. The exception was near Dickson where a severe thunderstorm produced large hail as it raced northeastward towards the Kentucky border.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Twitter user reported penny size hail on Guthrie Highway near Clarksville.

Drought conditions worsened the most for counties near the Tennessee River and those along and near the Cumberland Plateau. For much of the month, precipitation totals were well below normal, generally ranging from 1 to 3 inches. A beneficial storm system brought widespread rainfall during the last couple days of November that should help improve drought conditions in the next updates in December.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe drought conditions developed across Perry County in November 2022.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Clusters of thunderstorms tracked east-southeast from Middle Tennessee and Kentucky during the afternoon hours of September 25. There were numerous reports of wind damage across the northern Cumberland Plateau and northeast Tennessee counties, mainly in the form of downed trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple large trees were blown down. A tree that was blown over hit a vehicle traveling on Shady View Road near Kingsport, killing a woman.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave moved from western Arkansas into the Mid-South and interacted with a moist and unstable airmass across the Delta region. Thunderstorms developed with several reports of damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and powerlines were down across Covington. Significant damage was caused to temporary structures and tents in the Town Square, which were set up for the Heritage Festival. Flying debris damaged downtown structures and broke windows at some local businesses.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong winds occurred across the Tennessee Valley ahead of a cold front. Some locations had trees and limbs blown down, resulting in some power outages. Behind the front, temperatures dropped quickly, with some locations recording wind chill values of -15 F or lower. Some snow accumulations were also reported, mainly in the mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust to 43 mph was measured at the KCHA ASOS.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After drought conditions worsened during an abnormally dry fall, a more active pattern set up during the end of November and into early December. This led to rapidly improving drought conditions, and by December 13th, only far northwest Stewart County was in moderate drought. Rainfall totals across Middle Tennessee generally ranged from 4 to 7 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe drought conditions from November 2022 improved to no drought conditions in December.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Bitterly cold temperatures and wind chills arrived behind a very strong Arctic cold front.  Wind chill values stayed zero or below for around 36 hours straight.  In some cases, wind chills dropped into the -15 to -24 degree range in parts of southern middle Tennessee.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The wind chill dropped into the 0 to -10 degree range.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Bitterly cold temperatures and wind chills arrived behind a very strong Arctic cold front.  Wind chill values stayed zero or below for around 36 hours straight.  In some cases, wind chills dropped into the -15 to -24 degree range in parts of southern middle Tennessee.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The wind chill remained in the 0 to -10 degree range.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A highly anticipated strong Arctic Cold front arrived during the evening hours, which not only brought the onset of an intense bitterly cold wave for the next few days, but also produced a surge of strong and damaging wind gusts along the front. The wind gusts and damage were much like occurs along a warm season squall line. But in this case, a line of showers and a significant atmospheric pressure rise with the passage of the cold front contributed to the strong and gusty winds. In some cases, the winds exceeded 40 mph, then subsided into the 30 to 35 mph range.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An initial peak wind gust of up to 46 mph, was followed by wind gusts of 30 to 40 mph for several hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Light snow developed during the mid afternoon hours of the 26th, and ended during the early evening hours. Snowfall amounts from a dusting to just under an inch were reported. Due to very cold road surface temperatures, traffic on area roadways resulted in melting and immediate refreezing of moisture on roadways creating icy and impassible road conditions.  Improvement occurred rapidly by late morning on the 27th as temperatures warmed well above the freezing mark.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light snow fell during the late afternoon into the early eveninghours. Accumulation was less than an inch.  However, due to the recent bitterly cold weather, road surface temperatures were also very cold. The melted snowfall on traveled roadways refroze into black ice very quickly. This resulted in severe travel conditions and disruptions.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light snow fell during the late afternoon into the early eveninghours. Accumulation was less than an inch.  However, due to the recent bitterly cold weather, road surface temperatures were also very cold. The melted snowfall on traveled roadways refroze into black ice very quickly. This resulted in severe travel conditions and disruptions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stalled warm front and plentiful amounts of atmospheric moisture helped to set the stage for a persistent wet pattern that lasted several days in early December across Middle Tennessee. By December 7, 2022, an estimated 2 to 4 inches over the course of this event had begun to cause creeks to overfill their banks. Minor flooding was reported across areas mainly south of Interstate 40, particularly in the Spring Hill area where several flooding reports were received along with road closures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Persistent rainfall which was heavy at times forced the closure of McLemore Avenue in Spring Hill due to flooding over the roadway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An abnormally strong and cold upper-level low pressure system plunged southward through much of the central and eastern United States, bringing a blast of Arctic air. These historically cold temperatures impacted all of Middle Tennessee beginning late on December 22, 2022. As the Arctic front swept eastward through the area, temperatures plummeted with low temperatures on the morning of December 23rd in the single digits and even below zero. This front also brought strong winds, and the combination of very cold air and strong winds resulted in brutal wind chills with values of -15 degrees to -30 degrees common areawide through December 24th. While snow was observed with this system, it did not last long and snow accumulations of only 1 to 2 inches was found approximately in areas north of Interstate 40. However, this snow resulted in significant impacts by early December 23rd with numerous traffic accidents and road closures reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A weather station near Short Mountain measured a minimum wind chill of -26 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mixture of freezing rain, sleet and snow occurred in southern middle Tennessee. Light snow and ice accumulations were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain mixed with and changed to snow. Ice accumulations of 0.1-0.2 inches and snowfall of less than 0.5 inch were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the late afternoon into the evening hours on 1/2/2022, an area of low pressure over southern Mississippi moved northeast along a stalled front extending northeast into central Alabama. As this occurred it wrapped very cold air around the surface low into northern/central Alabama into Tennessee producing a quick changeover from rain to moderate/heavy snowfall as the surface low moved quickly into northern Georgia. Temperatures remained near or just below freezing during the late evening and overnight hours. This produced a band of heavy snowfall west of the surface low over northern Alabama into southern middle Tennessee. Four to seven inches of snow fell near and northeast of the I-65 corridor before snowfall ended. Elsewhere, widespread snowfall amounts of 1 to 4 inches were experienced.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three to seven inches of snowfall was reported across the county. Six inches of snowfall was reported at the Sewanee COOP site in the eastern portion of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level trough and attendant cold front rotated through the region during the afternoon and evening of October 12, 2022. The cold front interacted with a moist and unstable airmass to produce numerous thunderstorms across West Tennessee and north Mississippi. Several storms were severe with large hail and damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and power lines were knocked down across Saltillo, Pickwick, Big Ivy, and Gillis Mills areas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system tracked east across the deep South on the 16th producing rain and snow across the Tennessee Valley.  Temperatures in valley locations in southern middle Tennessee dropped to or just below freezing by the mid afternoon hours on the 16th, but likely much sooner atop the Cumberland Plateau including the Sewanee area where 5 inches of snow was measured.  In lower elevations of Franklin County, 2 to 3 inches was measured.  In Lincoln and Moore County, 1 to 2 inches of snow was measured.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall accumulation of 2 to 5 inches fell above 1400 feet MSL elevation, while valley locations received an inch or less.  Sewanee observed 5 inches of snow accumulation at the University of the South.
